translation.content: To the casual onlooker, Enzo Siragusa may seem easy to pigeonhole as just a UK DJ playing tech house. Wrong. The Maidenhead native has serious pedigree, earned from 30-plus years fully immersed in the rave, first as a dancer at jungle and hardcore parties, then as a hobby DJ in local bars and finally as the cofounder of one of London's most influential club nights: Fuse. What began life in 2008 as a ramshackle afterparty on Brick Lane is now a global brand with multiple festivals and roving events, and Siragusa is its figurehead. For years, Fuse's MO was doggedly down to earth, built on a love for intimate shows where the residents took top billing. Its impact on modern dance culture is clear to see in the runaway success of the so-called UK tech house movement (think Michael Bibi, PAWSA and Chris Stussy). And yet Fuse always cut deeper, darker and dubbier. "I felt you could bring the emotion of jungle and hardcore into minimal house, which is what became the Fuse sound," Siragusa told fabric last year. This style defined Fuse for a decade-plus, though for those paying close attention there were always hidden layers to Siragusa's DJing. A child of Metalheadz as much as Perlon, his tastes—and vast vinyl collection—run wide, and today the breadth of these influences inform his creativity behind the decks more than ever. As RA.1008 illustrates, he now plays less minimally without sacrificing any of the all-important edge, quirkiness or emotional hooks. Spanning nearly two hours, the mix powers through dazzling tech house, minimal, speed garage, jungle and more. The blending is sublime, each new transition a window into a fleeting sonic world. There's no tracklist—many of the tunes are unreleased and even unnamed—but we managed to snag at least one gem to exemplify the riches on show: Adam Marshall's 2014 piano warbler "Thelon." Good luck IDing the rest. -Carlos Hawthorn What have you been up to recently? It's been a great summer. We've had the usual run of festival touring and played some incredible shows: Wildeburg, Hide & Seek and Glastonbury were real highlights. With Fuse, it's been a particularly inspiring season. Our Open Air in London was a special moment, followed by an amazing Australia tour, Off Sonar at Monasterio, and our first-ever Malta festival. That one was a real milestone—three days with a lineup we were able to fully curate, bringing together some of our absolute favourites like Ricardo Villalobos, DJ EZ and Kerri Chandler, alongside all our regulars (Traumer, The Ghost, Sweely, Sonja, Apollonia, Rossi). It's been busy, but honestly, such a fun and motivating summer. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? The mix was recorded at my friend's spot, Cult & Coffee listening bar in Salford, Manchester. I don't currently have a home setup, and all my records apart from four bags are in storage due to my house build, so this was the perfect place to do it. It took a good few takes to get it right. It definitely wasn't smooth sailing all the way through, but we got there in the end. There wasn't a huge conceptual idea behind the mix; it's just an honest reflection of my influences and style, keeping it true to how I'd usually play at my own parties. You'll hear everything from deep house and minimal to tech house, jungle / breaks and a bit of speed garage in there, too. Anyone who's been to my more intimate shows will know I like to take people on a journey through different genres and energies. If anyone can tell me what I play in fewer words I'd love to know! There are quite a few unreleased bits in the mix, alongside a couple of classics that never leave my bag, like Hanna's "Time (Doza's Lawnchair Generals Mix)" and Adam Marshall's "Thelon"—tracks I've been playing and have loved for many years. The overall idea was to capture a version of me that you'd experience at certain Fuse parties or during an extended set, where I can really stretch out and show the full spectrum of what I love to play. The success of Fuse helped supercharge the UK tech house sound that's now filling festivals and arenas worldwide. What did you make of the scene when you started out, and what do you make of it today? It means a lot to me to have inspired a couple of generations of new producers and DJs over the years. The scene today feels very different to when I first started out. Back then, everything was fresh—there were distinct scenes, and you'd naturally jump between drum & bass, garage and house. Now, it's a different landscape, but I feel incredibly grateful to still be part of it and to be able to reach more people than ever. There are so many ears tuned into this sound now, but at the core, nothing's really changed. I still get to play the records I love and hopefully expand people's minds in the process. When I used to go out, I was always searching for something deeper, something I could really feel and connect with. It's amazing to see that the people on the dance floor today are still searching for that same feeling. Being able to facilitate their journeys through my DJ sets, productions and the events I create is something that means a great deal to me. Your peers (and Fuse regulars) like Chris Stussy, Apollonia and Laidlaw are some of the most surefire draws in 2025. What do you think is the formula helping them cut through? I think if you've got real talent, combined with the right passion, work ethic and desire, you’ll cut through. When it comes to bringing artists through our events or labels, I've always looked for qualities beyond just musical ability. Breaking through in this scene is incredibly tough, and at Fuse, myself and the team really try to recognise both the talent and the individual behind it. Chris [Stussy] is a great example. We've worked closely with him and watched him grow into one of the best DJs in the world, and it's been amazing to be part of that journey. Laidlaw is another fine artist—someone who started out as a graphic designer (as well as a DJ) with Fuse. We saw his creative mind, his talent and his appetite to succeed, and he's gone on to prove he can do incredible things, with plenty more still to come. The likes of Apollonia are another perfect example. They've stood the test of time, pushed past the initial hype and remain just as relevant today. They're three brilliant DJs and people—exactly the kind of acts you want to be associated with. Fuse has also expanded its bookings to include artists like Ben UFO, Shanti Celeste and Dr Banana. Tell us about the programming ambitions for 2026 and beyond. When it comes to programming Fuse, we've always tried to find balance and expand our musical palette creatively, without preconceptions. Over the years, a few outlets have pigeonholed us as exclusively a tech house brand—which is easy to do—but we see music as constantly evolving and don't like to limit ourselves to exclusively booking one type of artist or specific niche (much like the mix and our own influences). We love working with artists who are masters of their craft. Ben [UFO] is a great example. Well over a decade ago, we used to have Rinse FM shows next to each other, so our paths crossed often. We've known each other a long time, but the public doesn't always see the behind-the-scenes relationships that shape some of the programming decisions. Meeting and knowing people on a personal level and enjoying their art—beyond what's fed through social media—is a huge part of how we curate events. For us, it's not just about ticket sales; it's about respecting the craft, the heritage and the music. We like to book DJs who are exceptional at what they do, while also balancing emerging artists who are connecting with a younger generation. It's an opportunity to give artists the chance to engage with different generations and styles of DJs, and also showcase different sides of themselves and their sound at high-quality events. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? At the same time, there are so many conflicts happening globally, and I feel like political agendas and the media, including social media, are creating a divisive world. Everywhere you look, politics are driving people further and further apart. I've always found comfort, love and connection in music and in meeting people from all kinds of backgrounds. As a first-generation immigrant, it's painful to see people being judged or attacked because of their race, religion, sexuality or any other factor. What I've always loved about the rave scene is that it brings people together from all over the world, with different backgrounds, without judgment. None of us can control the circumstances we're born into: our name, where we're from, the religion we're raised in. I just wish more people could approach the world with the open-mindedness and acceptance that exists in our scene. There's a track I included in my mix that really struck me a few years ago. It's by DJ Adnan, a half-Palestinian, half-Brazilian artist, called "Stop War."
translation.blurb: A joyride through rave-ready tech house, speed garage, jungle and more.
